data.title: Elon Musk says recent X outage issues demand 'major operational improvements'
data.description:
• Outages on the Elon Musk-owned social media platform X in recent days are an indication that "major operational improvements" need to be made.
  • "The failover redundancy should have worked, but did not," Musk wrote in a Saturday morning post on X. 
  • Earlier Saturday, Downdetector indicated a spike in user-reported outages. By 1130a ET, the number of outages had dropped dramatically.
  • On May 22, a data center in Hillsboro, Ore., that X leases experienced a fire.
  • On Friday night, X's engineering team said that they were still experiencing issues related to the fire and "there may be delays in notifications and Premium features."

data.title: Chevron may receive minimum maintenance license for Venezuela oil operations
data.description:
• The U.S. government is prepared to issue Chevron (NYSE:CVX) a limited license for its oil operations in Venezuela that would allow for necessary upkeep and safety activities.
  • The development, reported by Bloomberg, comes after U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio on May 21 said that Chevron's license to operate in Venezuela would expire on May 27. 
  • The news service noted it is unclear whether similar licenses are under consideration for other oil companies with operations in the heavily sanctioned country.

data.title: Insider trades: Microsoft, AMD, Walmart among notable names this week
data.description:
A weekly overview of insider trading activity, highlighting notable buy and sell transactions by investors, directors, and executives. The following trades occurred or were notified between May 19 and May 24.

  • Microsoft’s (NASDAQ:MSFT) EVP and Chief Commercial Officer Judson Althoff sold 16,000 shares of the tech giant at around $458 apiece for a total value of $7.3M. Althoff ownership has reduced to about 98,010 shares following the transaction. Meanwhile, a second executive vice president at Microsoft, Kathleen Hogan sold 21,500 shares for $454 each, totaling $9.8M. Hogan maintains an ownership of 134,887 shares in the company.
  • Walton Family Holdings Trust divested a sizeable stake in Walmart (NYSE:WMT) worth $741M. It sold 7.5M shares in the company in the price range of $96.6 to $98.6. It still retains a considerable ownership of the company of about 574M shares.
  • David Glazer, CFO at Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) offloaded 34,230 shares of the firm in the price range of $125.2 to $127.7, totaling $4.3M. Glazer still retains ownership of nearly 5M shares of the company. Other official of the company, including Chief Revenue Officer Ryan Taylor and Chief Accounting Officer Jeffrey Buckley also reduced their holdings in the company this week.
  • Rafael Marquez, president of international card services at American Express (NYSE:AXP),  slashed his stake in the company by $3.6M. Marquez sold 12,00 shares in the company for $297 each. His ownership has now reduced to 8,078 shares in the company.
  • Brendan Nelson, senior vice president at Boeing (NYSE:BA), revealed in a filing this week that he let go of 640 shares of the planemaker for 206.28 each, for a total value of $132,019. He currently owns 13,258 shares of the company.
  • On the buy side, AMD’s (NASDAQ:AMD) EVP and Chief Commercial Officer Philip Guido bought 8,800 shares of the company for $113.56 each, resulting in a total investment of nearly $1M. Guido’s total holdings in the company now stand at 42,322 shares.
  • Paul Middleton, CEO of Plug Power (NASDAQ:PLUG) purchased 350,000 shares of the company for $0.72 each for a total value of $250,390. Middleton’s stake in the company has now jumped to nearly $2M.

data.title: EPA draft plan would end limits on greenhouse gases from power plants - report
data.description:
• A plan drafted by the Environmental Protection Agency would eliminate current limits on greenhouse gas emissions from gas and coal-fired power plants.
  • A draft of the document was sent to the White House on May 2, The New York Times reported. It could be released for public comment in June.
  • The plan argues that greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide emitted from power plants "do not contribute significantly to dangerous pollution" or climate change as it accounts for a declining portion of emissions worldwide, the Times said, quoting from the document. The EPA added that ending such emissions wouldn't have a significant effect on public health and welfare.
  • In April 2024, the Biden administration unveiled new restrictions on coal-fired plants. At that time, the EPA said the restrictions would reap significant public health benefits, including up to 1,200 premature deaths, 870 hospital visits, and 1,900 cases of asthma.

data.title: Sezzle, IREN climb, HASI, WD slide in week's biggest financial movers
data.description:
Financial stocks fell with the broader stock market in a low-volume week ahead of the Memorial Day weekend.

Overall, the Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) slid 3% for the week ended May 23, 2025, compared with the S&P 500's 2.6% decline.

Falling the most of any financial stock (with market cap over $2B) this week, HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (HASI), dropping 11.4%;

Walker & Dunlop (WD) gapped down 10%;

TransUnion (TRU), a consumer credit reporting agency, shed 9.5%;

TPG (TPG), which during the week priced a secondary public offering, also retreated 9.5%; and

MARA Holdings (MARA) rounded out the five biggest losers with a 9.1% slump, even as bitcoin (BTC-USD) rose to new record highs during the week.

On the winners, technology-enabled payment company Sezzle (SEZL) jumped 12.3%;

From there, bitcoin miner IREN (IREN) gained 8.4%;

South Korea-based KB Financial Group (KB) ascended 7.1%;

Shinhan Financial Group (SHG), a fellow South Korean lender, accelerated 7.1%; and

U.K.'s Lloyds Banking Group (LYG) advanced 7%.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Netflix, UnitedHealth and GE Aerospace among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500)closed in the red, after the week saw investors focusing on President Trump's "One Big Beautiful" tax bill, which was narrowly passed by the U.S. House.

Trump also said tariffs must be paid on iPhones not made in the U.S. and also threatened a 50% tariff on the European Union. For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) fell 1.5%, while Dow (DJI) declined over 2%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

GE Aerospace downgraded on valuation concern

GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) was downgraded to Neutral from Buy by Northcoast Research, citing concerns about the jet-engine maker’s valuation following a 36% rally in just a few weeks.

The brokerage also pointed to macroeconomic risks and an increasingly murky outlook for the aerospace sector.

“We prefer to sit this one out, looking for the U.S. trade impasse to be ironed out and the risk of reduced aviation industry profitability to subside,” analyst Chris Olin said, adding that there are “just too many unsolved questions surrounding the aero peer group for us to keep buy ratings in place.”

Netflix risk/reward appears balanced, says JPM

J.P. Morgan downgraded Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) to Neutral from Overweight, citing a more balanced risk/reward scenario following its "significant" stock price appreciation and outperformance.

JPM views NFLX as "highly defensive" against tariff and macro concerns. But if tariff and macro pressures continue to ease, the brokerage expects investment dollars to rotate from defensive names to those that have been more vulnerable and under pressure.

NFLX's PT was hiked to $1,220 from $1,150, saying that JPM expects NFLX to benefit from the global proliferation of Internet-connected devices and increasing consumer preference for on-demand video consumption over the Internet.

Google's AI search potential poses threat for Reddit, says Wells Fargo

Wells Fargo downgraded Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) to Equal weight from Overweight, citing changes in search traffic trends up ahead as Google ramps up AI search capabilities. The stock’s PT was cut down to $125 from $168.

“As we expect Google to more aggressively pursue this AI search transition, we see greater uncertainty with RDDT," noted Wells Fargo.

The social media platform is expected to see impact for logged-in user growth and high-margin data licensing revenue as a result of changing search trends, Wells Fargo said, adding that it thinks the company is unlikely to fulfill market expectations for advertising growth on logged-in users.

UnitedHealth downgraded on earnings growth concerns

UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) was downgraded to Reduce from Hold by HSBC Global Research, citing concerns related to leadership change, pulled 2025 guidance, and alleged Medicare fraud.

The brokerage also cut PT for the managed care giant to $270 from $490, saying that although newly appointed CEO Stephen Hemsley has the opportunity to start on a cleaner slate, there's a risk to earnings growth along with policy overhang.

“We also see potential recovery getting delayed, as reflected in our 2026-27e estimate cuts,” the analysts said.

Loop Capital reiterated its Buy rating on Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), noting that Blackwell's autumn ramp up expectations will carry the stock. The firm has a $175 price target ahead of the AI chip giant's first quarter (April) fiscal 2026 results on May 28.

Oppenheimer upgraded Charter Communications (NASDAQ:CHTR) to Outperform after the company announced a merger deal with Cox Communications, having an enterprise value of $34.5B.

Morgan Stanley downgraded Asana (NYSE:ASAN) to Underweight from Equal-weight, citing its premium valuation amid rising competitive pressures and internal challenges. Similarly, Deutsche Bank downgraded KLA (NASDAQ:KLAC) to Hold from Buy.

data.title: D-Wave CEO says US trails other nations in this key quantum technology
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum (NYSE:QBTS) made waves this week with the launch of its new Advantage2 quantum computing system for general availability. 

Shares for the quantum computing company surged more than 50% over the past five sessions. Shares are up 124% year to date.

In an interview with Seeking Alpha, D-Wave CEO Alan Baratz highlighted the differences between his company and competitors. Namely, that D-Wave systems utilize quantum annealing while most rely on gate-based quantum computing.

"There are a lot of big companies building quantum computers," Baratz said. "We are the only ones using annealing. Others are using gate-based quantum computing. The annealing allows us to do much more than gate-model systems. As a result, they are being used more today."

IBM (IBM), Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), IonQ (IONQ) and Rigetti (RGTI) are examples of companies developing gate-based quantum computing. 

Quantum annealing involves "solving optimization problems by encoding the problem into the energy levels of a physical system and then letting the system evolve towards the global minimum of the energy landscape," according to Quantum Zeitgeist. Meanwhile, "gate-based quantum computing is a method of performing quantum computation by applying a sequence of quantum gates to a set of qubits."

Quantum annealing is generally considered less likely to commit certain errors, such as noise and decoherence.

Several customers are already achieving promising results through use of the Advantage2. For example, scientists at Los Alamos National Laboratory are using it for new discoveries in condensed matter theory and magnetic materials. Davidson Technologies is developing quantum optimization applications designed to support mission-critical challenges and national security-focused quantum research. Japan Tobacco found the Advantage2 can accelerate its efforts in achieving quantum AI-driven drug discovery. 

"I believe the U.S. government is behind other governments on annealing," Baratz said. "They have nothing for annealing systems, and nothing focused on near-term applications that could benefit the public today. It could be used for anything from troop resupply optimization to shipping port throughput. It could reduce delays for hurricane evacuations and assist in wildfire management. There is a broad array of challenging problems the public sector needs to address."

Despite the importance of quantum computing for research institutions, the majority of D-Wave's current customers are commercial clients.

"We have over 130 customers today," Baratz said. "We are the only quantum computing company with that level of usage. Look at IonQ, they have a handful of customers. The same is true for Rigetti. Over 70 of our customers are commercial. Of the 70, 25 of them are Forbes Global companies. The rest are research or educational institutions. With each quarter that goes by, we add new customers."

Trevor Lanting, chief development officer for D-Wave, said the advances with each new generation of annealing quantum computers are many times over their predecessors. 

"Each new generation of systems we are developing are not mere marginal increases," he said. "They are creating foundational new use cases that weren't even possible before ... It is absolutely comparable to what the large hyperscalers are doing. You can enhance machine learning models. You can improve the reliability of these models, and there is some exciting early research."

"We've increased qubit coherence," Lanting added. "It drives better performance and creates a growing set of use cases for end customers. We have an eye to addressing real-world use cases."

Despite solving complex problems at a much faster rate, quantum computing also requires much less energy usage than classical computing.

Advantage2 is available now in more than 40 countries through D-Wave's quantum cloud service. Applications for their system can be built using the Python programming language. GitHub currently carries many examples.

data.title: Construction of Venture Global's CP2 LNG plant in Louisiana approved by U.S. regulator
data.description:
The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission have given permission for Venture Global (NYSE:VG) to proceed with construction of its CP2 liquefied natural gas plant in Louisiana, Reuters reported Friday.

The decision followed a final environmental study that shows the 28M metric tons/year plant is in the public interest.

Venture Global (NYSE:VG) had obtained approval to build the plant, but after a court ruling, FERC conducted an additional environmental review of the impact on air quality, which concluded that the project should be allowed to continue.

With federal approvals in hand, Venture Global (VG) will immediately launch on-site construction for the project, CEO Mike Sabel said.

If completed, CP2 would be the single largest LNG export facility in the U.S. and could make Venture Global (VG) the largest U.S. LNG company with 66.5M tons/year, surpassing Cheniere Energy's (LNG) expected 60M tons/year of capacity by that time.

data.title: SoCal Edison to pay record $82.5M settlement for sparking 2020 Bobcat fire
data.description:
Edison International's (NYSE:EIX) Southern California Edison said Friday it agreed to pay $82.5M to settle claims with the U.S. Forest Service for costs and damages related to the 2020 Bobcat Fire in the Los Angeles area.

The U.S. had filed a lawsuit against SoCal Edison in 2023, alleging negligence that caused the wildfire, which burned nearly 180 square miles and became one of Los Angeles County's largest and most expensive fires ever.

The settlement will help the federal government recoup costs of battling the fire, which was estimated to have cost more than $100M.

"This record settlement against Southern California Edison provides meaningful compensation to taxpayers for the extensive costs of fighting the Bobcat Fire and for the widespread damage to public lands," U.S. Attorney Bill Essayli said.

As part of the agreement, Edison (NYSE:EIX) did not admit fault or any wrongdoing.

data.title: TotalEnergies CEO defends company against abuse allegations in African project
data.description:
TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) CEO Patrick Pouyanne defended the company's work on oil projects in east Africa at its annual shareholders meeting Friday, Reuters reported, after a United Nations human rights representative said the company needs to address fresh allegations of abuses.

Special Rapporteur on Environmental Defenders Michel Forst said TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) had failed to take effective steps to address abuses against activists linked to the $15B East African crude oil pipeline project and connected oilfields.

Also, Germany's Union Investment said it dropped French company from its sustainability funds and called for an independent human rights audit following abuse allegations related to the project.

TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) denied the allegations concerning the Kingfisher oil site in Uganda, part of the East African crude oil pipeline project in which the company holds a 62% stake.

"We develop these projects with the values and principles that are ours, we are convinced of the good they do for the countries and the local populations," Pouyanne said, adding that projects such as the UGanda site are better done by TotalEnergies (NYSE:TTE) than others, who may care less about human rights and the environment.
